feedback.text: As a lifetime consumer of digital content, I have witnessed and suffered the rise of malpractice from major actors over the years, who have made the creative landscape, as well as the consumer experience, exponentially worse over the years. What used to be free out of the box has been made a service available for purchase. What could easily have been made a single-purchase offer then is now fragmented into battle passes, with the additional risk to the consumer to not have access to the entirety of the product they have bought if they do not invest additional time putting in the work towards making their purchase available for them, usually fighting against an unreasonable deadline, forcing people into a vicious cycle of addiction. Ads have become atrocious traps for gambling addicts, as well as children, minorities, or otherwise vulnerable people. Mobile games, and their subsequent imitations, are a major cause for financial instability in less educated households, namely due to their ease of access for children, or the lack of additional security put in place for people too old or not educated enough to see through the cleverly disguised scams, posing as easy money grabs. Furthermore, products sold have now become ephemeral. Within the span of 20 years, games have stopped being property. Before, you could own a disk and play it as long as it was physically intact. Now, most games bought are completely inaccessible to the people who spent money on acquiring a copy if the studio behind them decides to pull the plug, due to the idea of games as a service. This leads to legally gray actions, such as emulation, to access games that are otherwise unavailable. To me, this sounds like art destruction, and punishing people for daring to step in a museum where long lost pieces have been recreated so humanity can witness them again. This is entirely unethical, and should not be allowed. A property once sold should belong to its buyer, and any decision made by the maker of said property should have no right to effectively forbid and outlaw the owner's access to their property on a whim, with no alternative to enjoy their property. Even worse, this barring of access to owned property has led to the rise of "Classic" games, where studios sell versions previously made unavailable at full retail price, effectively selling the customer what they should always have had access to a second time, aided by the psychologically manipulative narrative of giving people the opportunity to relive the experience they fondly remember from years ago, and legally attacking anyone who might have supported such versions in free-of-charge manners in efforts of conservation, only to pile on more profit. I am thinking of World of Warcraft: Classic as the worst offender, or even Dofus: Classic, Everquest, so on, so forth. Projects that, by all means, should have been left to the public after having been considered obsolete, or made available for free, instead of being allowed to be sold a second time. It should be forbidden to allow studios to force game owners out of their property. It should be forbidden to allow studios to sell games that have been made unavailable under new branding, and higher pricing, with virtually no difference to the original product. It should be mandatory for studios to have plans, set in motion immediately after the decision is made, to allow the owners of a game's copy to still be able to keep their property functional and useful after end of support has been enacted, regardless of the copy being digital or physical. This is a clear violation of customer rights that has gone far too long unpunished, due to the relatively recent nature of video games. It is important to punish bad actors and prevent their proliferation, in the interest of digital media, the digital industry, as well as anyone that interacts with it, on all sides of the spectrum, whether developer or costumer.
